lookup_context: Domestic en route CPDLC uplink
Controller needs an altitude assignment
Instruction is to descend to an assigned altitude and maintain it
items.task: Select the structured altitude uplink family.
items.instruction: Use the domestic CPDLC altitude uplink table.
items.question_text: Which table entry family fits the described CPDLC altitude instruction?

choices.text: UM20 climb-to-and-maintain altitude.
UM23 descend-to-and-maintain altitude.
UM135 confirm assigned altitude.
UM177 pilot discretion only.

items.explanation: TBL 14-2-6 separates climb, descent, confirmation, and pilot-discretion functions. A descent to an assigned altitude and maintain instruction is the UM23 family.

lookup_context: Domestic en route CPDLC uplink
Crossing fix restriction
Restriction contains both altitude and speed
items.instruction: Use the domestic CPDLC crossing-constraint table.
items.question_text: Which crossing-constraint entry is the best source match?

choices.text: CROSS (position) AT (time).
CROSS (position) AT (speed).
CROSS (position) AT AND MAINTAIN (altitude) AT (speed).
MAINTAIN BLOCK (altitude) TO (altitude).

items.explanation: TBL 14-2-8 has separate crossing rows for time, speed, altitude, and combined altitude-plus-speed restrictions. The combined altitude and speed case uses the combined crossing-constraint entry.

items.situation: A controller needs to send a domestic CPDLC route clearance, and a structured route uplink entry in the CPDLC route table matches the clearance. A trainee suggests sending it as free text because the wording would be quicker to type.

choices.text: Use the matching structured route uplink entry instead of free text.
Use free text whenever it is faster than selecting a structured element.
Use an emergency/urgency message because route changes affect safety.
Use a downlink table because the pilot will acknowledge the clearance.

items.explanation: Free text is for content that does not conform to a standard message element. When a structured CPDLC element fits, the source-use decision should favor that structured element.

lookup_context: Oceanic CPDLC uplink
Altitude change begins at a specified waypoint
Instruction is climb to and maintain
items.task: Select the correct altitude uplink pattern.
items.instruction: Use the oceanic CPDLC altitude uplink table.
items.question_text: Which altitude uplink family fits this clearance?

choices.text: AT (position) CLIMB TO AND MAINTAIN (altitude).
AT (time) DESCEND TO AND MAINTAIN (altitude).
REPORT GROUND TRACK.
REQUEST BLOCK (altitude) TO (altitude).

items.explanation: TBL 14-3-6 distinguishes immediate altitude changes from time- or position-triggered changes. A climb beginning at a waypoint uses the position-triggered climb-to-and-maintain pattern.

lookup_context: Oceanic CPDLC downlink
Pilot requests a weather deviation
Deviation is up to a specified distance and direction off route
items.instruction: Use the oceanic lateral downlink table.
items.question_text: What is the correct source-use interpretation of this downlink?

choices.text: A pilot request for weather deviation off route.
An ATC clearance to deviate off route.
A report that the aircraft is back on route.
A system-management connection message.

items.explanation: TBL 14-3-5 is the lateral downlink table. A REQUEST WEATHER DEVIATION message is a pilot request, not the ATC uplink clearance.

items.situation: An oceanic aircraft is offsetting around weather and later sends BACK ON ROUTE. Another trainee treats the message as a new request for an offset clearance.
items.question_text: How should the message be classified?

choices.text: As a lateral downlink report that the cleared route has been rejoined.
As a lateral uplink clearance to start an offset.
As a route uplink direct-to clearance.
As a free-text emergency message.

items.explanation: In TBL 14-3-5, BACK ON ROUTE reports that the aircraft has rejoined the cleared route. It is not an offset request or a controller-issued clearance.

lookup_context: Successive departures from the same or adjacent airports
Courses diverge by at least 45 degrees
Divergence occurs within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff
items.question_text: For this DME/ATD-based diverging-course case, what minimum applies until courses diverge?

choices.text: 1 minute.
2 minutes.
3 miles.
5 miles.

items.explanation: When courses diverge within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff, the listed minimum is 3 miles until courses diverge.

lookup_context: Terminal diverging runway departures
Compare nonintersecting and intersecting runway cases
Runway divergence is 15 to 29 degrees inclusive
items.task: Identify the additional condition for the intersecting-runway case.
items.question_text: What extra condition applies to the 15-to-29-degree intersecting-runway case?

choices.text: The preceding aircraft has commenced a turn after passing the runway intersection.
The runways must be at least 3,500 feet apart at all points.
The aircraft must remain on the same heading for 5 minutes.
Both aircraft must use DME/ATD distance separation.

items.explanation: For intersecting runways diverging by 15 to 29 degrees inclusive, the preceding aircraft must have passed the intersection and commenced a turn.

lookup_context: Same, converging, or crossing courses
Leading aircraft is at least 22 knots faster
Conditions for the 22-knot family are met
items.question_text: What minimum applies in the 22-knots-or-more family?

choices.text: 5 miles DME/ATD or 3 minutes between other aircraft.
10 miles DME/ATD or 5 minutes between other aircraft.
20 miles DME/ATD or 10 minutes between other aircraft.
30 miles in all cases.

items.explanation: When the leading aircraft is at least 22 knots faster and a listed condition is met, the applicable minimum is 10 miles for DME/ATD cases or 5 minutes between other aircraft.

lookup_context: One aircraft is climbing or descending through another aircraft's altitude
Compare DME/ATD distance option with timed option
Sequence and altitude-change timing matter
items.instruction: Use the altitude-crossing longitudinal separation figures.
items.question_text: Which sequence geometry supports the altitude-crossing minimum?

choices.text: The descending aircraft is leading, or the climbing aircraft is following.
The descending aircraft must always be following.
The climbing aircraft must always be leading.
Aircraft sequence does not matter if both aircraft report the same fix.

items.explanation: The altitude-crossing figures depend on sequence: the descending aircraft is leading or the climbing aircraft is following. Timed separation adds the listed 4,000-foot and timing conditions.

lookup_context: Departure using an intersecting runway
Another aircraft is using or has used the intersecting runway
Projected runway or flight path conflict must be protected
items.task: Choose the operational test before takeoff roll.
items.instruction: Use the intersecting runway departure figures.
items.question_text: What must be true before the departure begins takeoff roll?

choices.text: The preceding aircraft has passed or avoided the intersection conflict, or the preceding arrival has met a listed clear, hold-short, exit, or passed-intersection condition.
The preceding aircraft has merely accepted a clearance.
The following aircraft is ready for immediate departure.
The controller expects the preceding aircraft to clear before liftoff.

items.explanation: The intersecting runway figures protect the intersection or intersecting flight path. Takeoff roll waits until one of the listed conflict-resolving conditions exists.

lookup_context: Arrival using an intersecting runway or intersecting flight path
Another aircraft is departing or arriving on the protected path
Traffic information and runway/flight path protection are required
items.task: Select the protection needed before the arrival crosses threshold or flight path.
items.instruction: Use the intersecting runway arrival figures.
items.question_text: Which condition protects the arriving aircraft's threshold or flight path?

choices.text: The preceding departure has passed the intersection/flight path or is airborne and turning to avert conflict, or the preceding arrival is clear, holding short, or past the intersection/flight path.
The preceding aircraft has been issued traffic information only.
The following arrival is on final and expects to land before the other aircraft reaches the intersection.
The aircraft are on different frequencies.

items.explanation: The arrival figures focus on protecting the threshold or intersecting flight path before the arriving aircraft crosses it. Traffic information alone does not establish the required separation condition.

lookup_context: Simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs
Two landing or takeoff points
Courses to be flown must not conflict
items.question_text: What minimum spacing is required between landing or takeoff points?

choices.text: At least 200 feet.
At least 700 feet.
At least 1,500 feet.
At least 3,000 feet.

items.explanation: Simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs require at least 200 feet between points and nonconflicting courses.

lookup_context: Sea-lane arrival separation
Arrival follows a preceding aircraft still relevant to the sea lane
Suitable landmarks allow distance determination
items.task: Choose the controlling basis for the minimum.
items.question_text: What controls the minimum distance needed before the arriving aircraft crosses the landing threshold?

choices.text: The aircraft categories and whether the preceding aircraft landed or departed.
Only the surface wind direction.
Only whether the operation is IFR.
Only whether the aircraft is turbine powered.

items.explanation: Sea-lane arrival distances vary by aircraft category and by whether the arrival follows a landed aircraft still in the sea lane or an airborne departure before the sea-lane end.
